source: Model answers + marking key quality conclusions

quality_justification_framework.principle: Quality level must be justified by specific tasting observations. It is NOT an opinion — it must follow from evidence.

excelente.example_reasoning: Outstanding quality: pronounced intensity, complex layered aromas across primary (red cherry, raspberry), secondary (vanilla, cedar), and tertiary (earth, leather, forest floor); high acid and medium tannin in balance; long finish; clearly developing.

muy_bueno.example_reasoning: Very good quality: medium(+) intensity, complexity from primary fruit, secondary oak/MLF (biscuit, vanilla), and tertiary development (honey, nutty); full body; medium(+) finish; clear regional style expression.

bueno.example_reasoning: Good quality: well-expressed primary character; some secondary complexity; balanced palate; medium finish.

aceptable.example_wine: Ca di Alte Pinot Grigio / 16 Stops Shiraz
aceptable.example_reasoning: Acceptable quality: simple; primary fruit aromas; clean; straightforward palate; short-medium finish; no secondary or tertiary complexity.

reasoning_patterns_observed_in_model_ans.example: Very good quality. [implicit justification from preceding tasting notes]
reasoning_patterns_observed_in_model_ans.note: In model answers quality level is stated as conclusion; justification is implied by the complete tasting notes above

reasoning_patterns_observed_in_model_ans.example: The wine is of a very good quality.
reasoning_patterns_observed_in_model_ans.note: Brief, declarative; marks awarded for correct level selection

common_quality_errors: Selecting 'excelente' for a simple wine because it is personally enjoyed
Not recognising 'simple' as a valid and mark-earning descriptor for basic wines
Selecting 'muy bueno' for a wine with only primary aromas (should be 'bueno' or 'aceptable')
Selecting 'bueno' when wine clearly shows all three aroma categories and long finish (should be 'muy bueno' or 'excelente')
